title: Microscopic Origin of Emergent Elliptic Flow and Molecule Formation in Strongly Interacting Quasi-Two-Dimensional Few-Body Systems
authors: Xin-Yuan Gao, Kai Yuen Lee, Qingze Guan, Yangqian Yan
abstract: Recent experiments simulating two-dimensional few-fermion systems have observed emergent hydrodynamic behavior, i.e., interaction-driven elliptic flow by adding fermions two at a time [S.~Brandstetter et al., Nat. Phys. (2025)]. Due to the curse of dimensionality and strong correlations, capturing such phenomena beyond two particles remains challenging. Here, we use the ab initio time-dependent explicitly correlated Gaussian (TDECG) method to quantitatively reproduce these experimental observations. With only a moderate number of correlated Gaussian basis functions, our approach obtains converged dynamical observables for systems up to six particles. Furthermore, real-time access to the many-body wavefunction and two-point correlation functions enables us to visualize the transformation from a strongly interacting gas to a stream of paired molecules, i.e., a dynamical BCS-BEC crossover.
summary: This work develops and applies the Time-Dependent Explicitly Correlated Gaussian (TDECG) method to simulate strongly interacting few-fermion systems in 2D. It successfully reproduces key experimental signatures, including interaction-driven elliptic flow and the BCS-BEC crossover, by tracking the formation of molecular pairs. The development also rigorously compares variational principles, establishing the Lagrangian approach as the most numerically stable tool for these complex dynamics.
main_problem: The paper addresses the microscopic origin of emergent hydrodynamic behavior, specifically elliptic flow and molecule formation, in strongly interacting quasi-two-dimensional few-fermion systems.
main_result: The TDECG method successfully reproduces the experimentally observed elliptic flow and demonstrates a dynamical BCS-BEC crossover, providing a microscopic picture of chemical freeze-out.
method: The study employs the Time-Dependent Explicitly Correlated Gaussian (TDECG) method, utilizing a Lagrangian variational principle for stable, real-time evolution of the many-body wavefunction.
model_or_system: The system consists of spin-balanced N fermions in an anisotropic 2D harmonic trap, interacting via a short-range potential. The analysis covers few-body configurations up to N=6, examining the transition from a gas to molecular pairs.
key_observables: Elliptic flow ($\delta r_x/\delta r_y$), BCS-BEC crossover signatures (momentum distribution $C^{(2)}_R(p_R)$), and the pair-relative probability density.
important_parameters: The scattering length ($a_{2D}$) and effective range ($r_e$) are tuned using a double-Gaussian pseudopotential to match experimental parameters.
main_assumptions: The primary assumption is the applicability and convergence of the TDECG method, which is shown to be numerically superior to the McLachlan formulation for long-time dynamics.
figures_summary: Figures illustrate density snapshots showing flow evolution, momentum distributions confirming pairing, and quantitative comparisons of the aspect ratio and pair-relative density against theoretical bounds.
paper_structure: The paper details the general variational framework, the model Hamiltonian, the ECG ansatz, and the numerical implementation, emphasizing the derivation and comparison between the Lagrangian and McLachlan variational principles for stable time evolution.
why_interesting: The combination of advanced numerical techniques (TDECG) with the study of fundamental quantum phenomena like BCS-BEC crossover and hydrodynamic flow in ultracold atomic systems makes this highly relevant to AMO physics and quantum gases.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1. Few-fermion 2D expansion: density snapshots, TDECG convergence, and emergence of elliptic flow. (a)- (e) One-body densities for N = 3+3 at ωt = 0, 5, 30 in real space and ωt = 0, 5 in momentum space, with representative single- shot particle positions overlaid; black/white markers indicate sampled spin-up/spin-down particles. Dashed circles in (d),(e) mark the non-interacting Fermi momentum ℏkF ≃2.17 ℏ/aho of the 3 + 3 ground state in the anisotropic trap, corresponding to a Fermi energy EF = ℏ(5ωx + ωy)/2. (f) Momentum-space widths δpx/y(t) after trap release, showing interaction-induced anisotropy, for basis sizes Nb = 40 (dark, thick dots), 44 (normal, dashed lines), and 48...

Figure 2 reveals a striking particle-number dependence in the relative momentum correlator C(2) R . At ωt = 0, all three systems exhibit a pair of positive back- to-back lobes near |pR| ≈ 2ℏkF [arrows in panels (a),(e),(i)], characteristic of Cooper-pair correlations in which opposite-spin fermions occupy nearly opposite momenta close to the Fermi surface [41, 42]. By ωt = 1, these lobes have migrated inward toward pR = 0 [arrows in (b),(f),(j)], signaling the onset of molecule formation; the 3+3 system already develops a pronounced positive central feature, whereas the 1+1 and 2+2 systems still show a near-zero central value, indicating a slower crossover to the molecular regime. At long...

FIG. 2. Dynamical BCS-BEC crossover visualized through relative momentum correlations. Evolution of the relative momentum correlator C(2) R (pR) for the 1+1, 2+2, and 3+3 systems in the anisotropic trap, shown in the top, middle, and bottom rows, respectively. Red (green) regions correspond to enhanced (depleted) opposite-spin coincidence at relative momentum pR. From left to right, the four columns correspond to ωt = 0, 1, 5, 30. The dashed circles mark the eye-guide radius |pR| = 2ℏkF , and the black arrows in panels (a),(b),(e),(f),(i),(j) indicate the inward migration of the back-to-back positive lobes toward pR = 0.

FIG. 3. Quantitative evidence for molecular formation. (a) Aspect ratio δrx/δry: blue line (red circles) shows the full ECG many-body calculation (Monte Carlo sampling that treats the system as three distinguishable molecules). (b) Radial probability density of the pair-relative coordinate: orange circles show Monte Carlo sampling at ωt = 30; blue line shows the s-wave bound-state probability density |ψ0(r)|2 of the two-body Schr¨odinger equation with potential V (r), Eq. (4).

FIG. 1. Tunability of the 2D scattering length a2D and the effective-range parameter re for the double-Gaussian potential. (a) Potential shape for a single Gaussian (U1 = 0, blue dashed) and the double Gaussian with a potential barrier (U1 = 25.8ℏω, red solid), both with U0 = 104ℏω and σ = 0.1aho. (b) Effective-range parameter re for a single-Gaussian potential (U1 = 0) as a function of U0, where re remains strictly positive regardless of the value of U0. (c) Effective-range parameter re (left axis, red solid) and ln(a2D/aho) (right axis, blue dashed) for the double Gaussian as a function of U1 with fixed U0 = 104ℏω.
